questions.stem: A company needs to run a custom risk-scoring algorithm that doesn't map to any REST API. The code should execute in an isolated environment with no network access. Which ABL tool type should they use?

options.text: HTTP tool with a custom endpoint pointing to localhost
Code tool (sandbox) with the appropriate runtime
MCP tool connected to a local MCP server

questions.explanation: Code tools (type: sandbox) execute user-provided JavaScript or Python code in a gVisor-isolated environment with no network access and enforced resource limits. They are designed for calculations, data transformations, or ML inference that doesn't map to a REST API. HTTP tools require an external endpoint, and MCP tools connect to external MCP servers — neither provides an isolated code execution sandbox.

questions.stem: In ABL, secrets referenced in tool definitions use the placeholder syntax {{secrets.______}} and are resolved at runtime from the project's secret store.
questions.explanation: ABL uses the {{secrets.SECRET_NAME}} placeholder syntax to reference credentials. The runtime resolves these from the project's secret store at execution time. Actual credentials are never embedded in ABL files — only the placeholder name appears in the definition.

questions.stem: What is the role of the 'server' field in an MCP tool declaration?

options.text: It specifies the URL of the MCP server to connect to
It references a logical MCP server name configured in the runtime settings
It names the MCP transport protocol to use (stdio, http, or websocket)

questions.explanation: The 'server' field is a logical name that maps to an MCP server configuration in the project's runtime settings. Server configuration details — including transport type (stdio, http, websocket), connection URL, and authentication — are managed at the project level, not in the ABL file itself. This separation keeps ABL definitions portable across environments.

questions.stem: Code tool (sandbox) execution is gated by which mechanism?

options.text: A tenant-level feature flag (codeToolsEnabled) that defaults to false
A project-level permission that defaults to true for all plans
An agent-level EXECUTION property that must be set to sandbox: enabled

questions.explanation: Code tool execution is controlled by a tenant-level feature flag called 'codeToolsEnabled'. This flag defaults to false for all plans and must be explicitly enabled by a platform administrator before any sandbox tool will execute. When disabled, the runtime silently skips sandbox-type tools. This is a security measure to ensure code execution is opt-in.

questions.stem: To automatically map specific fields from a tool's response into session variables, you use the on_____: set: block in the tool definition.
questions.explanation: The 'on_result' block (with a nested 'set' property) allows you to automatically map tool result fields to session variables after successful execution. For example, 'on_result: set: customer_id: result.customer_id' copies the customer_id from the tool response into the session. There is also an 'on_error' block for handling failure cases.
